title: 第 50 節
en: jjpcoi hayi^ovai.
A a 2 356 LECTURE xiir.

half divine, could not but modify the whole character of the religion of the Greeks. Eeligion is, no doubt, a very general term, and it is difficult to lay hold of it when, as in ancient Greece, it existed as yet in the minds of individuals, or families, or villages only. On this account Hesiod's works, containing the first attempt at a systematic treatment of Greek religion, deserve to be considered by students of religion quite as carefully as the works of Homer. It may be useful, therefore, before we proceed further, to cast a glance at his Theogony, and at what he considered the objects of religious belief and worship to have been among the Greeks of his own time.

Whether the system of Greek religion which we find in Hesiod, was entirely his own work or the work of the Greek people at large, is difficult to say. It is quite certain, no doubt, that neither Homer nor Hesiod * made ' the gods of the Greeks, but it is truer of Hesiod than of Homer that he reduced them to something like order.

Hesiod admitted gods and men, but he also admitted intermediate beings. He seems to have been the first to use the word and the concept of 'rifjLiOeos, half-god, which was probably unknown to Homer (II. xii. 23). He applies it to those very heroes of the Trojan and Theban wars, of whom we have been speaking. But he never admits that an ordinary man could become a god or even a halfgod. Between gods and men he placed the heroes, ijpo)s, or half-gods, rjiiideoL, and the Daimones, 8at/xore9.
THE DIVINE AND THE HUMAN. 357 Heroes.

It is quite true, as I pointed out, that Hesiod's heroes were exceptional beings, not ordinary mortals. Yet the fact that these heroes were believed to have lived on earth, like ordinary mortals, and had, after their death, been transferred either to the isles of the blessed, or had been admitted to the society of the Olympian gods, made it easier in later times to imagine that a real mortal, distinguished above the rest, might share the same honours as those who were the direct descendants of the gods (ck Otcoi^ yeyovor^s, Isocr. 4, 84). We read of such men as receiving not only the usual ancestral worship, but as worshipped, like the heroes, and, in some cases, as worshipped like the gods. We read of the Chersonitae, for instance, sacrificing to Miltiades, the victor of Marathon, 490 b. c._, after his death, as to their founder (Herod, vi. 38). In Greece, where the patriotic feeling was so strong, nothing was more natural than that national honours should be paid to those who had died for their country. Pausanias tells us (i. 32, 4) that the Marathonians worshipped all who had fallen in the battle of Marathon, calling them Heroes, and especially Marathon, from whom their Demos had its name.

Plutarch relates (Arist. 21) that after the battle of Plataea, 479 B.C., the inhabitants performed funeral sacrifices [ivayiC^iv) every year to the Hellenes who had fallen and been buried there. And they do so still, he continues.

' On the 16th of the Attic month Mainiakterion they have a great procession, preceded at daybreak by a trumpeter who gives the sign to attack. Then follow carriages with myrtle and garlands, a black bull, and free-born boys, carrying libations of wine and milk 358 LECTURE XIII.

in amphoras, and oil and ointments in jugs. No slave is allowed to take any part, because those Greeks once died for freedom. At last follows the Archon of Plataea, walking through the town to the graves. He is never allowed to touch a sword or to wear any but white vestments, but on this occasion he is clothed in a red chiton, carrying an urn which he has taken from the archive, and girded with a sword. He takes water from a spring, washes the funeral columns himself, and anoints them with sweet oil. After he has killed the bull on the altar, and prayed to Zeus and to the chthonic Hermes, he invites the brave who have died for Hellas to the meal and the partaking of the blood. He then mixes a goblet of wine, and while pouring out the libation, he says : " I drink to men who died for the liberty of the Hellenes." This festival is kept by the Plataeensians to the present day.'

Thucydides (v. 11) tells us that ' Brasidas, who died after gaining the victory at Amphipolis (422 B.C.), was buried in the city with public honours in front of what is now the Agora. The whole body of the allies in military array followed him to the grave. The Amphipolitans enclosed his sepulchre, and to this day they sacrifice to him as to a hero, and also celebrate games and yearly offerings in his honour. They likewise made him their founder, and dedicated their colony to him.'

Many similar cases might be quoted, all showiug how the distance which originally separated men from the heroes as well as from the gods, became smaller and smaller in the eyes of the Greeks. When Demosthenes speaks of those who have fallen for their country, he says in so many words ^: 'How should one not consider men of such merit as blessed, men who have received such recognition and honour 1 One may fairly say of them that they are the assessors of the lower gods, and in the isles of the blessed placed in the same rank as the brave men of former times -.'
^ Demosth. epitaph. 1399, 36, p. 590, t. ii, Bekk. ' Lehrs, Populdre Aufscitze, p. 332.

THE DIVINE AND THE HUMAN. 359 Plutarch (Vita Lys. 18) tells us that Lysander (died 394 B.C.) was the first to whom the Greeks actually erected altars and offered sacrifices as to a god. In later times even bodily strength, athletic power, nay mere physical beauty, might raise a man to the rank of heroes. Herodotus tells us that Philip of Croton^ a contemporary of Lysander, obtained, on account of his beauty, what no one else had obtained, for the Egestans built a Heroion on his tomb, and honoured him by sacrifices (Ovo-ujcn). This custom was continued till at last ijpojs became the received name for any departed spirit. On Boeotian tombstones nothing is more common than the inscription, ijpai^ Xatpe, hail, O hero !

If then we find that an ordinary mortal could be honoured like a hero, and a hero could be worshipped like a god, it is easy to see that the mere likeness would soon be forgotten, that the mortal would be honoured as a hero, and the hero worshipped as a god. And thus a transition was efiected half-unconsciously between the human and the divine, and the existence of something divine in man would be admitted almost by necessity.
Daimones.

The conception of Daimones led by another road to the discovery of something divine in man. While the heroes were originally human, the Daimones were in the beginning purely divine beings with nothing human about them. Plutarch^ tells us that Hesiod ^ See Plut. Def. Orac. 10 : 'HcrtoSo? Sk KaBapws Koi Sicupiapiivajs vpuros h^iOrjKi Twv XoyiKwv rirrapa yivrj, Oiovs, elra dai/xovas noWovs KayaOovs, etra rjpwas, eira dvOpunrovs, rwv TjfiiOiojv ds T]p<x)as dtroKpidiVTOJV.
was the first who divided all rational beings into four classes, namely gods, daimones, many and good, heroes, including the half-gods, and men.

It may be doubtful whether Hesiod had this fourfold division always clearly before his mind, and whether it ever formed the recognised foundation of the religion of the Greeks. His identifying the daimones with the men of the golden race seems at all events purely arbitrary. This name of Daimones is in fact one of the most difficult names in the religious phraseology of the ancient Greeks. We translate the word by demons ^5 which gives of course a very imperfect idea of its import. At first batixoiv clearly meant a god, the same as Oeos. Thus we read in Homer of Athene returning from the earth to the Daimones on Olympus. Aalixoiv is used more especially when a god appears as an active power. It then comes very near to the Latin nuviien, a divine power, as distinguished from the more personal deus. Thus Helios, the sun, is called by Aristophanes a great demon among gods and men.
But we saw ah-eady how the same word haiixcjuv was used in another sense too. It was applied by Hesiod to the first or the golden race of men. This has caused much confusion.
Hesiod's Four Races.
Hesiod speaks of four races of men who lived before the present race, the golden, the silver, and the copper race, and lastly, the race of heroes or ?udfgods.
The first race, the golden, was made by the Olym- ' See H. Spencer, Sociology, p. 323.

THE DIVINE AND THE HUMAN. 361 pian gods under Kronos. They lived a life of perfect happiness ; and, without suffering from the evils of old age, they passed away as in a sleep. But after they had been buried, Zeus changed them into Daimones, and they now roam about on earth, clothed in air, and watching over laws and crimes.
The second or silver race was given up to folly, and neglected the worship of the gods. After passing away, they became the Blessed under the earth {kiTiyOoi LOL jjiCLKapes Oi'rjTol KokiovTai).

Then Zeus made the third or copper race, in the days when iron was not yet known. It is said that these men were made out of ash-trees (e/c \x^\iG>v), and it is curious to observe that the Germans also had a tradition that men were made out of such trees. They were warriors of enormous strength. After a time they died and passed away.

After these three races, the golden, the silver, and the copper races, we should expect the present or iron race. But here Hesiod interpolates the race of the heroes, the heroes of the Theban and Trojan wars, who are called by him i]ixi6eoi or half-gods. These, after they died, did not, as Homer represents them, descend into the house of Hades, but, according to Hesiod, Zeus allowed them to live in the isles of the blessed, ruled by Kronos as their king.

Daimon therefore meant originally god, and was probably on Greek soil as old a word as theos. Its etymology is unfortunately uncertain, but its meaning differs from that of theos chiefly by its impersonal and unmythological character. Daimones were divine agents, at first, without any proper names, without temples and sacrifices. Afterwards they were repre- 362 LECTURE XIII.
sented as the followers (Plat. Legg. viii. p. 848 d), the messengers, and the servants of the gods (Plut. Def. Orac. 13).

As these divine powers watch over man, it was supposed after a time that every man had his own daimon^ likewise every family, and every town. At first, these daimones were kind, tutelary beings. Hence the ayaOos haLjjLCdv, the good genius, of a man. But we also hear of an evil daimon, who is accountable for the misfortunes that befall a man. It is difficult for us to connect any clear ideas with these daimones who determined the fates of man. But how familiar this idea was to the Greeks, we see from such expressions as hatfjiovLos. A baLixovios avi]p was meant for a man possessed, strange, luckless, wretched, sometimes also for an inspired and marvellous person ^. Our awful comes often very near to baijjioi'LOs.
zh: 我無法翻譯您提供的文本。 「jjpcoi hayi^ovai」不是有效的英文原文，也不符合您所述《宗教學導論》(1873) 的學術內容特徵。 請提供： - 清晰的英文原文 - 完整的段落或句子 我將按照您的規則進行專業翻譯。
A a 2 356 講座第十四講
半神的性質，必然會改變希臘宗教的整個特質。誠然，宗教是一個非常籠統的術語，當它如同在古代希臘那樣，僅存在於個人、家族或村落的心靈中時，就很難把握住它。因此，赫西俄德的著作——包含了對希臘宗教首次系統性處理的嘗試——應當值得宗教學的學生們和荷馬的著作一樣仔細地加以研究。因此，在我們繼續之前，不妨先瞥一眼他的《神譜》，以及他所認為在他那個時代的希臘人當中，宗教信仰與祭祀的對象應為何物。
至於赫西俄德所呈現的希臘宗教體系是完全出自他本人的創作，抑或是整個希臘民族的共同成果，實難判斷。誠然可以肯定的是，無論荷馬或赫西俄德都不是「創造」了希臘的諸神，然而說赫西俄德將諸神整頓成某種秩序序列，比起荷馬而言更為恰當。
赫西俄德承認有神祇與凡人，但他也承認有中間性的存在。他似乎是最早使用「半神」(ἡμίθεος) 一詞及其概念的人，這個詞彙在荷馬那裡可能是不為人知的（《伊利亞特》xii. 23）。他將此概念應用於我們先前談論過的那些特洛伊戰爭與底比斯戰爭的英雄。但他從不承認一個普通人可以成為神祇，甚至成為半神。在神祇與凡人之間，他放置了英雄，ἥρωες，或半神，ἡμίθεοι，以及精靈，δαίμονες。
神聖與人類 357 英雄

誠如我之前所指出的，赫西俄德的英雄人物確實是特殊的存在，而非普通凡人。然而，正因為這些英雄據信曾如普通凡人般生活於人世，且其死後要麼被遷入福島，要麼被納入奧林匹斯諸神的行列，這就使得後世容易想像，一個傑出於眾人之上的真實凡人，可能會分享那些直接出自諸神後裔的英雄人物所獲得的同樣榮譽（ἐκ θεοί γεγονότες，伊蘇克拉底《第四篇演辭》，84 節）。我們讀到有此等人物不僅獲得通常的祖先祭祀，亦如英雄人物般受到祭祀，且在某些情況下，如諸神般受到祭祀。例如，我們讀到克里米亞人祭祀馬拉松戰役的勝利者米底亞得斯，在其死後如祭祀其開國功臣般進行祭祀（《希羅多德歷史》，卷六，第 38 節）。在希臘，民族愛國情感如此深厚，沒有什麼比為國捐軀者獲得國家榮譽更自然的事了。普桑尼亞斯告訴我們（第一卷，32 節，第 4 段），馬拉松人祭祀在馬拉松戰役中陣亡的所有將士，稱他們為英雄（Herōes），特別是祭祀馬拉松，他們的民區正是以此人之名命名。
普魯塔克記載（《亞里斯提德傳》第21章）在西元前479年普拉提亞戰役之後，當地居民每年都為埋葬在那裡的陣亡希臘人舉行葬祭儀式（ἐναγίζειν）。他補充說，他們至今仍在這樣做。
在雅典月份邁尼亞克特里翁月的16日，他們舉辦盛大遊行，黎明時由號手開路，發出進攻的信號。隨後是裝飾著桃金孃和花環的車隊、一頭黑色公牛，以及自由出身的少年兒童，他們攜帶著葡萄酒和牛奶的奠祭 358 第十三講

在陶罐中盛放，油和膏油則裝在罐子裡。任何奴隸都不得參與其中，因為那些希臘人曾為自由而死。最後是普拉提亞的執政官穿過城鎮走向墳墓。他被禁止接觸劍，也不得穿著白色以外的衣服，但在這個場合，他穿著紅色長袍（chiton），攜帶從檔案室取出的骨灰罈，並佩戴著劍。他從泉水中汲取清水，自己洗淨葬禮紀念柱，並用香油為其塗抹。在祭壇上宰殺了公牛後，向宙斯（Zeus）和地下之神赫耳墨斯（chthonic Hermes）祈禱，他邀請為希臘而死的英勇者參加宴飲和血液的分享。隨後他混合一杯葡萄酒，在倒出奠酒時，他說：「我敬酒給那些為希臘人自由而死的人。」普拉提亞人至今仍保持著這個節慶。
修昔底德（v. 11）告訴我們：「布拉西達斯在安菲波利斯戰役中取勝後死亡（西元前422年），並以公開的榮譽被埋葬於該城市內，地點就在現今的廣場（Agora）前方。整支盟軍以軍事陣列跟隨他前往墓地。安菲波利斯人圍繞他的墳墓而建，直到今日他們向他獻祭如向英雄獻祭，並在他的名義下舉辦競技和每年的祭祀。他們同樣將他視為城市的建立者，並將他們的殖民地奉獻給他。」
許多相似的案例都可以舉證，全都展示了最初將人類與英雄、乃至與諸神區隔開來的距離，在希臘人眼中如何逐漸縮小。當德摩斯坦尼（Demosthenes）談及那些為國犧牲的人士時，他直言^：「怎能不視這等功績卓著的人們為有福者，這些人獲得了如此的表彰與榮譽呢？公平地說，他們是下位眾神的評議員，在極樂島嶼上與往昔勇敢的英雄們列為同等的地位。」
^ 德摩斯提尼《悼詞》1399，§ 36，第 590 頁，第二卷，貝克版本。¹ 萊爾斯，《大眾論文集》，第 332 頁。

普魯塔克（《呂山德傳》18）告訴我們，呂山德（卒於西元前394年）是第一位希臘人為其實際豎立祭壇並向其獻祭（如同向神祇獻祭）的人。在後世時代，即使是身體的力量、運動的能力，乃至於單純的身體美貌，也可能將一個人抬升至英雄的地位。赫羅多德告訴我們，克羅頓的菲利普——呂山德的同時代人——因其美貌而獲得了他人未曾獲得的榮譽，因為埃格斯坦人在其墓上建造了英雄廟（Heroion），並以獻祭（θυσίαι）來尊敬他。這一習俗延續下去，最後 ἥρως 成為了對任何亡靈的通用名稱。在比奧提亞的墓碑上，沒有比「ἥρως χαῖρε」（祝安，英雄啊！）的銘文更常見的了。
倘若我們發現一個普通凡人可以像英雄一樣受到尊敬，而英雄可以像神祇一樣受到崇拜，那麼不難看出，單純的相似性將很快被遺忘，凡人將作為英雄而受到尊敬，英雄將作為神祇而受到崇拜。由是，在人與神聖之間的轉變便半無意識地實現了，而人身中存在神聖之物的存在幾乎勢在必然地被承認了。
戴蒙（Daimones）

「提婆」(Daimones) 的觀念以另一條路徑導向了對人身上神聖事物的發現。雖然英雄人物原本是人類，但提婆在最初乃是純粹的神聖存在，身上毫無人性。普盧塔克告訴我們赫西俄德 ^ ^ 參見 Plut. Def. Orac. 10 : 'HcrtoSo? Sk KaBapws Koi Sicupiapiivajs vpuros h^iOrjKi Twv XoyiKwv rirrapa yivrj, Oiovs, elra dai/xovas noWovs KayaOovs, etra rjpwas, eira dvOpunrovs, rwv TjfiiOiojv ds T]p<x)as dtroKpidiVTOJV.
首先將所有理性存在者分為四個等級，即諸神、精靈（daimones）、眾多而善良的英雄（包括半神），以及人類。

海西俄德是否始終將這四分法清晰地銘記在心，以及它是否曾經成為希臘人宗教公認的基礎，這都是可疑的。他把達蒙（daimones）與黃金時代的人相提並論，至少似乎純屬武斷。達蒙這個名字實際上是古代希臘宗教用語中最困難的名字之一。我們將這個詞翻譯為「惡魔」，當然這只能給出其含義最不完整的概念。起初 δαίμων（daimōn）明確指稱一位神祇，與 Θεός（theos）相同。我們在荷馬的著作中讀到雅典娜從地上返回奧林匹斯山的達蒙。δαίμων 尤其在神祇作為一股活躍的力量出現時被使用。它接近拉丁文的 *numen*——一股神聖的力量，與更具人格性的 *deus* 相區別。因此，太陽神赫利俄斯被阿里斯托芬尼稱為神祇與人類之間的偉大達蒙。
但我們已經看到，同一個詞彙 haiixcjuv 也在另一個意義上被使用過。赫西俄德將其應用於人類的第一代或黃金種族。這引起了許多混淆。
赫西俄德的四個種族
赫西俄德談到在現今人類種族之前，曾有四個人類種族存在過，即黃金種族、白銀種族、青銅種族，以及最後的英雄種族或半神種族。
第一個種族，黃金種族，是由奧林匹亞人創造的。
神聖與人性。361 皮安諸神在克洛諾斯統治之下。他們過著完全幸福的生活；不遭受衰老的苦難，如同在睡眠中一樣逝去。但在他們被埋葬之後，宙斯將他們變成了精靈（Daimones），他們現今漫遊於大地上，身披空氣，監視律法與罪行。
第二代或白銀種族沉溺於愚昧，怠慢了對諸神的祭祀。在逝去之後，他們成為了地下的有福之人（makariōi khthonioi kalountai）。
然後宙斯創造了第三個種族，或謂銅人種，那是在鐵尚未為人所知的年代。據說這些人是從白臘樹（ἐκ μελιῶν）造成的，有趣的是德國人也有一項傳統說人類是由此類樹木造成的。他們是力量巨大的戰士。經過一段時間後，他們死亡並消逝了。
在這三個種族——黃金種族、白銀種族和青銅種族——之後，我們應該期待現在的或鐵種族出現。但在這裡，赫西俄德插入了英雄種族，即底比斯戰爭和特洛伊戰爭的英雄，他稱他們為 *ἡμίθεοι*（半神）。根據赫西俄德的說法，這些英雄死後並不像荷馬所描繪的那樣下降到哈迪斯之宅，而是由宙斯允許他們居住在福樂島上，由克洛諾斯作為他們的國王統治。
因此，**Daimon**（δαίμων）原初意指神靈，在希臘語境中可能與 **theos**（θεός）同樣古老。其詞源學遺憾地難以確定，但與 **theos** 的意義差異主要在於其非人格性與非神話性格。**Daimones**（δαίμονες）最初是沒有專名、沒有廟宇與祭祀的神聖媒介。其後它們被賦予……
作為諸神的追隨者（Plat. *Legg.* viii. p. 848 d）、使者與僕人（Plut. *Def. Orac.* 13）而被呈現。

弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒 由於這些神聖力量監護著人類，後來人們便認為，每個人都有自己的守護神（daimon），同樣地，每個家族與每個城鎮也都有其守護神。起初，這些守護神（daimones）是仁慈的護佑者。因此，一個人的「善靈」（ἀγαθὸς δαίμων，即良善的守護神）便由此而來。但我們也聽聞有邪惡的守護神，祂們對降臨於人身上的不幸負責。對我們而言，要將清晰的概念與這些決定人類命運的守護神（daimones）聯繫起來，實屬困難。然而，從δαιμόνιος（受守護神影響的）這類用語中，我們便可看出此觀念對希臘人而言是多麼熟悉。一個δαιμόνιος ἀνήρ（受守護神影響的人）意指一個著魔的、古怪的、不幸的、可悲的人，有時也指一個受靈感啟發而令人驚奇的人。我們的「awful」（令人敬畏的）一詞，往往與δαιμόνιος（受守護神影響的）意義十分接近。
